summary: Gruber acknowledges that Windows 8's new touch-based UI, inspired by Windows Phone 7, shows genuinely innovative thinking with its tile-based interface and snap multitasking. However, he argues that Microsoft's strategy of layering this new touch UI on top of the existing Windows desktop is fundamentally misguided. He contends that Microsoft doesn't understand why Apple built iOS as a separate OS rather than adapting Mac OS X for touch. The iPad succeeds precisely because it eliminated the complexity of traditional computing rather than hiding it behind a touch shell. Gruber argues that Apple maintains two separate platforms — Mac OS X for complex tasks and iOS for simplicity — because you cannot design one system that serves both paradigms. Windows 8's attempt to merge both worlds will fail because it carries 25 years of Windows baggage.
key_points: Windows 8's Metro-style tile UI and snap multitasking show genuinely innovative thinking, particularly the tiles concept which suits larger screens well
Microsoft is fundamentally wrong to build their next-gen touch interface on top of existing Windows rather than creating a clean-break OS like Apple did with iOS
The iPad succeeds because it eliminated complexity rather than covering it up — running full desktop apps on a touchscreen makes the experience worse, not better
Apple maintains Mac OS X alongside iOS specifically so iOS can remain lightweight; the Mac carries the baggage so iOS doesn't have to
Nokia, HP, and Microsoft are all now following Apple's lead in mobile computing, demonstrating the massive industry impact of iOS
money_quotes: Microsoft is obviously trying to learn from Apple, but they clearly don't understand why the iPad runs iOS, and not Mac OS X.
The ability to run Mac OS X apps on the iPad, with full access to the file system, peripherals, etc., would make the iPad worse, not better.
The iPad succeeds because it has eliminated complexity, not because it has covered up the complexity of the Mac with a touch-based 'shell'.
You can't make something conceptually lightweight if it's carrying 25 years of Windows baggage.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
Apple's radical notion is that touchscreen personal computers should make severely different tradeoffs than traditional computers — and that you can't design one system that does it all.
They can make buttons more 'touch friendly' all they want, but they'll never make Excel for Windows feel right on a touchscreen UI.
key_insight: Microsoft's fatal error with Windows 8 is believing you can graft a touch interface onto a desktop OS, when Apple's success with iOS proves that touchscreen devices require a clean break from legacy computing — eliminating complexity rather than disguising it.
